Issues.Title: Fix flaky test: retry binlog copy on IOException
Issues.Body:
## Summary

Fixes #54817

The `File.Copy` for binlog upload to Helix can throw `IOException` when MSBuild hasn't fully released the file handle, causing test failures unrelated to the test logic.

## Changes

- **New `FileUtility.TryCopyFile`** (`test/Microsoft.NET.TestFramework/Utilities/FileUtility.cs`): A reusable utility that copies a file with retry-on-IOException and diagnostic logging:
  - If the destination already exists, skips the copy and logs a warning
  - Retries up to 3 times with increasing backoff (500ms, 1s, 1.5s) on IOException
  - On final failure, logs a warning without throwing

- **Updated `TestCommand.Execute`** to use `FileUtility.TryCopyFile` for binlog uploads so transient file locks do not fail tests.

## Testing

Binlog upload is diagnostic-only — copy failures will not fail the test.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Fix flaky test: retry binlog copy on IOException" (#54831)

Issues.Title: Add Native AOT support for tool list --local, tool run, tool uninstall --local, and tool search
Issues.Body:
> **Stacked on #54810** (`baronfel/tool-resolution-aot`). This PR targets that branch so the diff under review is just the `dotnet tool` subcommand wiring. #54810 already makes tool-command **resolution/invocation** AOT-able (and links the shared manifest/package + command-resolution sources, grants `InternalsVisibleTo` for `IFileSystem`, and gates `IProject`/MSBuild off the AOT path); this PR builds on that to run the subcommands in-process. Once #54810 merges, this can be retargeted to `main`.

## Summary

Enables Native AOT support for the AOT-capable subset of `dotnet tool` subcommands: **local `tool list`**, **local `tool uninstall`**, **`tool run`**, and **`tool search`**.

Following the unified-parser model (#54653), `ToolCommandParser.ConfigureCommand` is shared between the managed and AOT paths via inline `#if CLI_AOT` regions, the AOT CLI links the **real** command implementations, and commands/options that cannot run under AOT throw `CommandNotAvailableInAotException` so `NativeEntryPoint` re-hosts the managed CLI.

## What runs under AOT vs. falls back

| `dotnet tool` invocation | AOT behavior |
| --- | --- |
| `tool list` / `tool list --local` | Runs in AOT (`ToolListLocalCommand`) |
| `tool uninstall <pkg>` (local) | Runs in AOT (`ToolUninstallLocalCommand`) |
| `tool run <cmd>` | Runs in AOT (`ToolRunCommand`) |
| `tool search <term>` | Runs in AOT (HttpClient + STJ source-gen) |
| `tool list --global` / `--tool-path` | Falls back to managed |
| `tool uninstall --global` / `--tool-path` | Falls back to managed |
| bare `tool`, `install`, `update`, `restore`, `execute` | Falls back to managed |

## Changes

- **`ToolCommandParser.cs`** — AOT-aware `ConfigureCommand`. Under `#if CLI_AOT` it only overrides the AOT-capable paths: local `list`/`uninstall` guard on `LocationOptions.IsGlobalOrToolPath` (global/tool-path throw fallback) and `run`/`search` call the real commands. Bare `tool` and `install`/`update`/`restore`/`execute` keep the recursive default fallback already set by `ConfigureAotActions`. The managed `#else` branch is unchanged from before.
- **`NugetToolSearchApiRequest.cs`** — under `#if CLI_AOT`, resolve the NuGet service index with `HttpClient` + a `System.Text.Json` source-generated context instead of NuGet.Protocol. The rest of the search pipeline (STJ deserializer, `PrintableTable`) was already AOT-friendly.
- **`Parser.cs`** — wire `ToolCommandParser.ConfigureCommand` into `ConfigureAotActions`.
- **`AotSourceFiles.props`** — link only the sources unique to running these subcommands in-process (the parser, the four command implementations, `PrintableTable`, and the NuGet search sources); the shared manifest/package and command-resolution infrastructure is already linked by #54810.
- **`AotParserTests.cs`** — parse coverage for the AOT-capable tool commands plus fallback assertions for the managed-only variants.

## Validation

- `dotnet-aot.csproj` and managed `dotnet.csproj`: build with 0 warnings / 0 errors.
- Full Native AOT publish (`-r win-x64`): ILC generates native code with **zero AOT/trim warnings**.
- `AotParserTests`: 38/38 pass (parse + managed-fallback theories, plus an invoke-level assertion that `tool list` executes on the AOT path without falling back).

## Performance

Measured with `eng/gather-otel.ps1` against the locally-built redist `dotnet.exe`, toggling `DOTNET_CLI_ENABLEAOT` (Debug, win-x64). End-to-end wall-clock per invocation:

`dotnet tool list` — fully handled on the AOT fast path (no network):

| | mean | median | min | max |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| managed | 378.2 ms | 374.9 ms | 363.8 ms | 417.2 ms |
| AOT | 68.4 ms | 67.6 ms | 64.4 ms | 72.8 ms |

→ **−309.8 ms, 5.5× faster, 81.9% lower latency** (12 timed runs/path, 2 warmups discarded).

`dotnet tool search` exercises the new `HttpClient` + STJ service-index path and returns identical results to the managed path. Its end-to-end time is dominated by NuGet network I/O (~700 ms of service-index + query round-trips), so the ~300 ms startup win is present but not the dominant cost (managed ≈ 875 ms vs AOT ≈ 922 ms mean, within network variance).
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Add Native AOT support for tool list --local, tool run, tool uninstall --local, and tool search" (#54827)

Issues.Title: Route verbose test output to file to reduce CI log size
Issues.Body:
## Summary

Follow-up to #54782. Addresses the remaining ~10 MB per shard caused by tests that intentionally invoke MSBuild with diagnostic verbosity.

## Problem

After #54782, three dotnet.Tests.dll shards remained at ~9.5-9.8 MB each. Root cause: ItUsesVerbosityPassedToDefineVerbosityOfConsoleLoggerOfTheTests runs dotnet test -v diag, producing ~95,000 lines of MSBuild output. Since the test expects exit code 1 (intentional failure), the dump-on-failure logic writes all of it to the test log.

## Solution

Added TestCommand.SuppressOutputOnFailure property:
- When set, command output is written to a file on disk instead of the test log on failure
- File goes to HELIX_WORKITEM_UPLOAD_ROOT (available for download) or temp
- CommandResult.StdOut/StdErr remain fully populated for assertions
- Test log shows a one-line pointer to the file location

Applied to the verbosity d and diag cases of the problematic test.

## Expected impact

~19 MB less per PR build run across the affected shards (from ~29 MB to ~10 MB for those 3 shards combined).

## Debugging

If you need the full output locally, set DOTNET_SDK_TEST_VERBOSE=1 to stream everything in real-time, or check the output file on disk / in Helix uploads.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Route verbose test output to file to reduce CI log size" (#54832)

Issues.Title: Centralize per-platform default build timeouts in sdk-build template
Issues.Body:
## Summary

Centralizes timeout logic in `eng/pipelines/templates/jobs/sdk-build.yml` so that each platform gets an appropriate default timeout without callers needing to specify one explicitly.

**Motivation:** The macOS ARM64 leg timed out at the 60-minute default ([build 1466643](https://dev.azure.com/dnceng-public/public/_build/results?buildId=1466643)). Rather than adding yet another per-leg `timeoutInMinutes` override, this makes the template platform-aware so all macOS legs automatically get the right timeout.

## Behavior

The template now applies variable defaults based on `parameters.pool.os`:

| Platform | Default timeout |
|----------|----------------|
| macOS    | 90 minutes     |
| All others | 60 minutes   |

Callers can still pass an explicit `timeoutInMinutes` parameter to override the defaults.

## Changes

- **`eng/pipelines/templates/jobs/sdk-build.yml`**: Replaced hardcoded 60-minute default with conditional logic based on `parameters.pool.os`
- **`.vsts-pr.yml`**: Removed now-redundant `timeoutInMinutes: 90` from macOS x64 CI-only leg
- **`.vsts-ci.yml`**: Removed now-redundant `timeoutInMinutes: 90` from macOS x64 official leg
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Centralize per-platform default build timeouts in sdk-build template" (#54804)

Issues.Title: Running file-based apps from $PATH can trip up on global.json in current directory
Issues.Body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem?

I used to write lots of small standalone tools in scripting languages like bash and python. Since file based apps, though, I am trying to start using .NET for the tools.

I created one called `~/bin/collect-npm-packages.cs` (`~/bin` is in my `$PATH`, meaning I can run this file directly by name). It look like any other script on Linux:

```
#!/usr/bin/env -S dotnet --

if (args.Length > 0)
...
```

And I run it as:

```
$ mkdir ~/test-directory
$ cd ~/test-directory
$ collect-npm-packages.cs .
```

And it works fine in the brand new directory called `test-directory`.

Then I tried to run this tool in a checkout of  https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/ (branch `release/9.0`):


```
$ collect-npm-packages.cs . 
The command could not be loaded, possibly because:
  * You intended to execute a .NET application:
      The application '--' does not exist or is not a managed .dll or .exe.
  * You intended to execute a .NET SDK command:
      A compatible .NET SDK was not found.

Requested SDK version: 9.0.118
global.json file: aspnetcore/global.json

Installed SDKs:
11.0.100-preview.4.26230.115 [/usr/lib64/dotnet/sdk]

Install the [9.0.118] .NET SDK or update [aspnetcore/global.json] to match an installed SDK.

Learn about SDK resolution:
https://aka.ms/dotnet/sdk-not-found
```

In other words, even though the file based app works fine when invoked directly in another directory, when it sees a `global.json`, it starts behaving differently. This makes it difficult to use lots of small file-based apps as generic tools.

This is similar to [dotnet run should ignore Directory.Build.props and Directory.Build.targets](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/52804) #52804

### Describe the solution you'd like

Is there some way to say "opt out of reading global.json"?



### Alternatives you've considered

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_

Issues.Title: Test It_throws_a_warning_when_NuGetPackageRoot_is_empty fails on release/10.0.3xx due to NuGet 7.6 behavior change
Issues.Body:
## Summary

The test `It_throws_a_warning_when_NuGetPackageRoot_is_empty` in `GivenThatWeWantToUseFrameworkRoslyn.cs` is consistently failing on all PRs targeting `release/10.0.3xx` for 11+ days. The test passes on `main`.

## Details

The test expects NETSDK1221 to be emitted when `NuGetPackageRoot` is empty during combined `/t:Restore;Build` with Full Framework MSBuild. However, on the `release/10.0.3xx` SDK (which bundles NuGet 7.6.0), `NuGetPackageRoot` is no longer empty at evaluation time — it appears to be pre-populated from the `NUGET_PACKAGES` environment variable that the test sets.

**On main** (SDK 11.x, NuGet 7.9.0): `NuGetPackageRoot` is empty at evaluation → warning fires → test passes ✅  
**On release/10.0.3xx** (SDK 10.0.3xx, NuGet 7.6.0): `NuGetPackageRoot` is NOT empty at evaluation → no warning → test fails ❌

Both use the same MSBuild 18.7.1 (VS 18 Insiders) on the Helix test machine. The SDK targets code is identical between branches.

## Evidence

- Build 1467087 (PR #54506, post-pin, Jun 16): FullFramework fails with this test
- Build 1457691 (PR #54506, pre-pin, Jun 10): Same failure
- Build 1465783 (PR #53297, unrelated backport): Same failure
- Main build 1468021 (Jun 17): FullFramework passes, this test passes
- Helix job `20d94c9e-9551-4fc7-9943-e24477f646cf` console log shows build succeeds with 0 warnings

## Root Cause Hypothesis

NuGet 7.6.0 (bundled in SDK 10.0.3xx) appears to initialize `NuGetPackageRoot` from the `NUGET_PACKAGES` environment variable during evaluation, which makes the property non-empty even for fresh projects with no `.nuget.g.props`. NuGet 7.9.0 (bundled on main) does not do this, preserving the old behavior where `NuGetPackageRoot` remains empty until `.nuget.g.props` is imported.

This means the underlying bug (issue #43016) may be fixed in NuGet 7.6.0, making the NETSDK1221 warning unnecessary in this scenario.

## Possible Fixes

1. **Update the test** to not set `NUGET_PACKAGES` env var (so `NuGetPackageRoot` is truly empty regardless of NuGet version)
2. **Condition the test** on the NuGet version behavior
3. **Remove the test** if the underlying issue is genuinely fixed by NuGet 7.6

## Context

- Original implementation: PR #43119 by @jjonescz (commit `05a82501a985`, Sep 2024)
- Linked issue: #43016 (also filed by @jjonescz)
- Follow-up fix: commit `e5f35a28bbfc` adding `-p:DOTNET_HOST_PATH=` args (Apr 2025, also @jjonescz)
- This is blocking ALL codeflow and backport PRs targeting `release/10.0.3xx`

/cc @jjonescz
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "Test It_throws_a_warning_when_NuGetPackageRoot_is_empty fails on release/10.0.3xx due to NuGet 7.6 behavior change" (#54828)

Issues.Title: dotnet test: surface Azure DevOps logging commands from MTP modules
Issues.Body:
## Summary

Under `dotnet test` (Microsoft.Testing.Platform), the SDK launches each test module with `RedirectStandardOutput = true` and captures its stdout, surfacing it only (buried) on failure. As a result, Azure Pipelines logging commands such as `##vso[task.logissue ...]` emitted by the `Microsoft.Testing.Extensions.AzureDevOpsReport` extension were swallowed and never reached the pipeline step's real stdout (which belongs to the `dotnet test` process), so failures did not surface on the Azure DevOps build summary.

## Change

When running on an Azure DevOps agent (`TF_BUILD=true`, honoring the `TESTINGPLATFORM_AZDO_OUTPUT=off|false|0` opt-out — matching Microsoft.Testing.Platform's own detection), the SDK now recognizes Azure Pipelines logging command lines (`##vso[...]` / `##[...]`) in the captured child stdout and re-emits them verbatim on its own stdout so they take effect. These command lines are excluded from the captured diagnostics buffer to avoid duplication.

This keeps the rich extension logic (quarantine, flaky demotion, repo-relative source paths, severity, etc.) authoritative — the SDK simply passes its output through rather than reimplementing it.

### Files
- `src/Cli/dotnet/Commands/Test/MTP/AzureDevOpsUtilities.cs` (new) — environment + logging-command detection
- `src/Cli/dotnet/Commands/Test/MTP/TestApplication.cs` — pass-through in the stdout reader
- `src/Cli/dotnet/Commands/Test/MTP/Terminal/TerminalTestReporter.cs` — `WriteAzureDevOpsLoggingCommand`
- `test/dotnet.Tests/CommandTests/Test/AzureDevOpsUtilitiesTests.cs` (new), `TerminalTestReporterTests.cs` — unit tests

## Notes / open question
This intentionally does **not** add a new `dotnet test` IPC message. Forwarding the extension's output over the pipe overlaps with in-flight Microsoft.Testing.Platform output-device work; the stdout pass-through is self-contained and works with the existing AzDO extension output as-is. If an IPC-forwarding protocol is preferred, the SDK consumer can be added once the message shape is agreed.

## Verification
- `dotnet.csproj` and `dotnet.Tests.csproj` build clean (0 warnings / 0 errors).
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "dotnet test: surface Azure DevOps logging commands from MTP modules" (#54812)

Issues.Title: Improve dotnet test (MTP) output handling: negotiate protocol 1.1.0 and forward IOutputDevice output
Issues.Body:
## Summary

Improves how `dotnet test` (Microsoft.Testing.Platform / MTP, pipe protocol) handles test-host output, building on testfx [#7564](https://github.com/microsoft/testfx/pull/7564) and addressing [dotnet/sdk#51615](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/51615).

The goal: **don't swallow the test host's console output.** When an extension/framework writes directly to the console it is now emitted live, and when it writes through MTP's `IOutputDevice` the text is forwarded to the SDK over the protocol so the `TerminalTestReporter` can render it cleanly (instead of being discarded or colliding with the SDK's own progress UI).

> [!NOTE]
> The host-side counterpart lives in testfx [#7564](https://github.com/microsoft/testfx/pull/7564) (advertises protocol `1.1.0`, stops plugging in its own `TerminalOutputDevice` under the pipe protocol, and forwards `IOutputDevice` text via the new `TestHostOutputDeviceMessage`). This SDK PR is the consumer side and is backward compatible with hosts that only speak `1.0.0`.

## What changed

**Protocol version negotiation**
- `ProtocolConstants.SupportedVersions` is now `"1.0.0;1.1.0"`.
- `GetSupportedProtocolVersion` now computes the real intersection of the host-advertised versions and the SDK-supported versions and returns the **highest common** `System.Version` (previously it did a single-string `Contains`, which could not negotiate multiple versions).
- The negotiated version is stored and exposed via `IsProtocol_1_1_OrHigher` so output behavior can branch on it.

**New IPC message — `TestHostOutputDeviceMessage` (serializer id 11)**
- Model + serializer + registration, byte-compatible with the testfx side (fields `ExecutionId=1`, `InstanceId=2`, `Text=3`).
- Routed through `TestApplication.OnRequest` → `TestApplicationHandler.OnOutputDeviceMessageReceived` → `TerminalTestReporter`.

**Live stdout/stderr (gated on negotiated `1.1.0`)**
- When `1.1.0` is negotiated, the child's stdout/stderr is streamed **live** through the reporter instead of being buffered and shown only on failure. A bounded tail (200 lines) is retained so the failure/handshake-failure summary still has recent context.
- Because the handshake can arrive slightly after the first output lines, output is buffered until the negotiated version is known, then flushed (live) or retained-for-failure-only.
- When only `1.0.0` is negotiated, behavior is **unchanged** (buffer, show on failure).

**Reporter passthrough**
- `TerminalTestReporter.WriteMessage` writes arbitrary passthrough text through the existing `TestProgressStateAwareTerminal.WriteToTerminal` mechanism, so the text is placed in the scroll area without corrupting the live progress region. It is reused for both live stdout and forwarded `IOutputDevice` text.

## Tests
- Serializer round-trip for `TestHostOutputDeviceMessage` (populated + null/empty fields; asserts id `11`).
- Protocol negotiation theory cases (highest-common selection, single-version, no-intersection, missing/empty advertised versions).
- `IOutputDevice` message forwarding renders text via the reporter; null/empty is a no-op.

All new tests pass locally (`dotnet.Tests`).

## Compatibility / notes
- Fully backward compatible: hosts advertising only `1.0.0` (or unknown serializer ids) are handled exactly as before; unknown messages are skipped.
- The testfx-side change must ship before the `1.1.0` path is exercised end-to-end.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Improve dotnet test (MTP) output handling: negotiate protocol 1.1.0 and forward IOutputDevice output" (#54825)

Issues.Title: Allow comments and trailing commas in launchSettings.json profile parsing
Issues.Body:
Fixes #54155 (and unblocks microsoft/testfx#7952).

### Problem
`dotnet run` fails to read a `launchSettings.json` that contains JSON comments or trailing commas:

```
The launch profile "(Default)" could not be applied.
An error was encountered when reading '...launchSettings.json': '/' is invalid after a value. ...
```

### Root cause
`LaunchSettings.ReadProfileSettingsFromFile` parses the whole file with a `JsonDocument` configured with `JsonCommentHandling.Skip` / `AllowTrailingCommas = true`, then hands the selected profile's **raw text** (`JsonElement.GetRawText()`, which still contains any comments) to the source-generated `ProjectLaunchProfileParser` / `ExecutableLaunchProfileParser`. Those deserializers used `LaunchProfileJsonSerializerContext` with no comment/trailing-comma handling, so they throw.

This is a regression introduced when profile parsing moved to source-generated deserialization.

### Fix
Add `[JsonSourceGenerationOptions(ReadCommentHandling = JsonCommentHandling.Skip, AllowTrailingCommas = true)]` to `LaunchProfileJsonSerializerContext`, matching the top-level `JsonDocument` options and the `dotnet-watch` launch settings reader.

### Tests
Added regression tests `CommentsAndTrailingCommas_Executable` / `CommentsAndTrailingCommas_Project` to `LaunchSettingsParserTests`. All `LaunchSettingsParserTests` pass locally.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Allow comments and trailing commas in launchSettings.json profile parsing" (#54820)

Issues.Title: Default Blazor WASM diagnostics support properties to true
Issues.Body:
The WASM runtime's `WasmFeatures.props` defaults `MetricsSupport`, `EventSourceSupport`, and `HttpActivityPropagationSupport` to `false` for size reasons. This silently breaks OpenTelemetry traces, metrics, and distributed tracing (`HttpClient` `traceparent` propagation) in Blazor WebAssembly apps unless developers manually opt in — a hard-to-diagnose failure mode, especially with .NET Aspire integration.

## Changes

- **`src/BlazorWasmSdk/Targets/Microsoft.NET.Sdk.BlazorWebAssembly.Current.props`**
  - Introduces a `BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled` property (default `true`) that gates enabling the three diagnostics switches.
  - Defaults `MetricsSupport`, `EventSourceSupport`, and `HttpActivityPropagationSupport` to `true` when diagnostics are enabled and the property isn't already set.

```xml
<BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled Condition="'$(BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled)' == ''">true</BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled>
<MetricsSupport Condition="'$(MetricsSupport)' == '' and '$(BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled)' == 'true'">true</MetricsSupport>
<EventSourceSupport Condition="'$(EventSourceSupport)' == '' and '$(BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled)' == 'true'">true</EventSourceSupport>
<HttpActivityPropagationSupport Condition="'$(HttpActivityPropagationSupport)' == '' and '$(BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled)' == 'true'">true</HttpActivityPropagationSupport>
```

## Behavior notes

- Properties are assigned in the `PropertyGroup` **before** the `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.WebAssembly` import, so the Blazor defaults win over the runtime's `'$(X)' == ''`-guarded defaults in `WasmFeatures.props`.
- Per-property `'$(X)' == ''` guards preserve explicit user overrides in `.csproj`.
- Opting out is a single switch: `<BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled>false</BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled>` restores the size-optimized runtime defaults; individual properties can also still be set explicitly.

The `BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled` name mirrors existing `Blazor*`-prefixed flags in the same file (e.g. `BlazorRoutingEnableRegexConstraint`).
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Default Blazor WASM diagnostics support properties to true" (#54824)

Issues.Title: Include resolved tool version in invalid DotnetToolSettings install/update errors
Issues.Body:
`dotnet tool install/update` could surface `DotnetToolSettings.xml` errors without indicating which package version was actually resolved, making tool-author diagnosis harder when version selection/ranges were involved. This updates the error path to include concrete package identity details.

- **Error payload now includes resolved package identity**
  - `MissingToolSettingsFile` now formats with package id + resolved version.
  - Message changed from a generic package-level error to:
    - `Settings file 'DotnetToolSettings.xml' was not found in package '{id}@{version}'.`

- **Version propagation at throw site**
  - In `ToolPackageInstance`, the missing-settings exception now uses the resolved `library.Version` (the concrete selected version), not just package id context.
  - Added a small helper to centralize formatting for this specific error path.

- **Focused unit coverage**
  - Added `ToolPackageInstanceTests` to assert the missing-settings message includes package identity in `<package>@<version>` form.

```csharp
throw new ToolConfigurationException(
    GetMissingToolSettingsFileMessage(packageId, library.Version));

internal static string GetMissingToolSettingsFileMessage(PackageId packageId, NuGetVersion packageVersion)
    => string.Format(CliStrings.MissingToolSettingsFile, packageId, packageVersion.ToNormalizedString());
```
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Include resolved tool version in invalid DotnetToolSettings install/update errors" (#54822)

Issues.Title: Blazor WebAssembly SDK should default MetricsSupport, EventSourceSupport, and HttpActivityPropagationSupport to true
Issues.Body:
## Summary

The WASM runtime's `WasmFeatures.props` defaults `MetricsSupport`, `EventSourceSupport`, and `HttpActivityPropagationSupport` to `false`. This causes OpenTelemetry (traces, metrics, logs) to silently fail in Blazor WebAssembly apps unless developers manually opt in via their `.csproj`:

```xml
<MetricsSupport>true</MetricsSupport>
<EventSourceSupport>true</EventSourceSupport>
<HttpActivityPropagationSupport>true</HttpActivityPropagationSupport>
```

The Blazor WebAssembly SDK should override these defaults to `true`, since Blazor apps commonly use `HttpClient` with distributed tracing, structured logging, and metrics — especially with .NET Aspire integration.

## History

- **Before .NET 9**: These switches either didn't exist or were effectively `true` (the linker didn't strip diagnostics infrastructure by default for WASM).
- **.NET 9 (December 2023)**: [dotnet/runtime#95486](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/pull/95486) introduced `WasmFeatures.props` which set `EventSourceSupport=false` and `HttpActivityPropagationSupport=false` as WASM defaults to reduce download size via aggressive linker trimming.
- **Later (.NET 9/.NET 10 timeframe)**: `MetricsSupport=false` was added to the same file.
- **.NET 10/.NET 11**: These defaults remain unchanged — any WASM app that references OTel packages gets silently broken unless it manually sets these properties.

The file currently lives at:
[`src/mono/nuget/Microsoft.NET.Workload.Mono.Toolchain.Current.Manifest/WasmFeatures.props`](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/blob/main/src/mono/nuget/Microsoft.NET.Workload.Mono.Toolchain.Current.Manifest/WasmFeatures.props)

## Problem

When a developer adds OpenTelemetry to a Blazor WebAssembly app:
1. **`MetricsSupport=false`** — `System.Diagnostics.Metrics.Meter` APIs are trimmed; OTel `MeterProvider` silently produces nothing.
2. **`EventSourceSupport=false`** — `EventSource`-based diagnostics are stripped.
3. **`HttpActivityPropagationSupport=false`** — `HttpClient` does not emit `Activity` objects or propagate `traceparent` headers, breaking distributed tracing.

All three fail **silently** — no exceptions, no warnings — making the issue extremely difficult to diagnose.

## Proposal

The Blazor WebAssembly SDK (`Microsoft.NET.Sdk.BlazorWebAssembly`) should set these defaults to `true` in its props/targets, overriding the low-level WASM runtime defaults:

```xml
<MetricsSupport Condition="'$(MetricsSupport)' == ''">true</MetricsSupport>
<EventSourceSupport Condition="'$(EventSourceSupport)' == ''">true</EventSourceSupport>
<HttpActivityPropagationSupport Condition="'$(HttpActivityPropagationSupport)' == ''">true</HttpActivityPropagationSupport>
```

### Rationale

- Blazor WebAssembly apps are **interactive web applications** that commonly make HTTP calls, use structured logging, and integrate with observability platforms.
- The aggressive trimming defaults make sense for bare `wasm` workloads (console apps, wasi, etc.) but not for Blazor apps where `HttpClient` and diagnostics are core scenarios.
- .NET Aspire's Blazor WASM integration (`Aspire.Hosting.Blazor`) relies on all three being enabled for the client service defaults to function.
- Developers who want smaller binaries can still opt out explicitly with `<MetricsSupport>false</MetricsSupport>`.

### Alternative

If changing the Blazor SDK default is too broad, the Aspire client service defaults template could set these — but that only helps Aspire users and doesn't fix the general "I added OTel to my Blazor WASM app and it doesn't work" experience.

## Related Issues

- [dotnet/aspnetcore#67259](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/issues/67259) — Circular DI in WASM ClientServiceDefaults
- [microsoft/aspire#18272](https://github.com/microsoft/aspire/issues/18272) — Gateway.cs circular DI with UseOtlpExporter
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "Blazor WebAssembly SDK should default MetricsSupport, EventSourceSupport, and HttpActivityPropagationSupport to true" (#54821)

Issues.Title: Flaky: RunFileTests_* (file-based program build) intermittently fail with MSB4025 'project file could not be loaded'
Issues.Body:
## Summary
The `Microsoft.DotNet.Cli.Run.Tests.RunFileTests_*` tests (file-based program / `dotnet run` & `dotnet build <file>.cs`) fail **intermittently** in CI. They are **not associated with any particular PR** — they fail across unrelated PR merge builds and on otherwise-clean changes.

## Affected tests (non-exhaustive)
- `RunFileTests_BuildCommands.Build_Library_MultiTarget`
- `RunFileTests_BuildCommands.Build_Exe_MultiTarget`
- `RunFileTests_BuildCommands.Pack` / `Pack_CustomPath`
- `RunFileTests_BuildCommands.NoBuild_02`
- `RunFileTests_CscOnlyAndApi.MSBuildGet_Consistent`

## Symptom
The generated file-based-program project is restored and then immediately reported missing:
```
<tmp>/Build_Library---XXXX/lib.cs.csproj : error MSB4025: The project file could not be loaded. Could not find file '<tmp>/Build_Library---XXXX/lib.cs.csproj'.
```
i.e. a race between generating/restoring the implicit `*.cs.csproj` and loading it (also seen as `No such file or directory` for the generated program, MSB4104). The failures are non-deterministic and clear on re-run.

## Impact
This is currently the dominant source of red CI on the MSTest-migration PR series and on unrelated PRs (observed in the majority of recent PR builds).

```json
{
  "ErrorMessage": "Microsoft.DotNet.Cli.Run.Tests.RunFileTests",

}
```

Issues.Title: Fix MSTest analyzer violations breaking main
Issues.Body:
## Problem
PR #54766 enabled the **Recommended MSTest analyzers** as build **errors**. PR #54758 merged ~2 minutes later, and its `Microsoft.TemplateEngine.Authoring.Tasks.IntegrationTests` code contains assertions that those analyzers now flag — so **main is currently red** and **every open MSTest migration PR is blocked** (their merge builds fail on these same errors).

## Fix
Resolve the analyzer violations in the two affected files (no behavior change):
- **`CommandResultAssertions.MSTest.cs`**: `MSTEST0037` (`Assert.IsTrue(a == b)` -> `Assert.AreEqual`; `Assert.IsFalse(a == b)` -> `Assert.AreNotEqual`) and `MSTEST0023` (`Assert.IsTrue(!x)` -> `Assert.IsFalse(x)`).
- **`LocalizeTemplateTests.cs`**: `MSTEST0037` (`Assert.AreEqual(n, x.Length)` -> `Assert.HasCount(n, x)`).

## Verification
- ✅ `Microsoft.TemplateEngine.Authoring.Tasks.IntegrationTests` builds clean (0 warnings / 0 errors) with the analyzers enabled.

This unblocks #54722 #54723 #54724 #54725 #54726 #54729 #54730 #54732 #54759 #54761 #54785 #54787.

Issues.Title: Clarify dotnet format include file filters
Issues.Body:
Addresses dotnet/format#634.

This clarifies how `--include` / `--files` interact with workspace loading:

- documents `--files` as an alias for `--include`
- explains that solution/project workspaces are still loaded before file filters are applied
- points whitespace-only single-file formatting to `dotnet format whitespace --folder --include ...`

Validation:
- `git diff --check`

Disclosure: I used OpenAI Codex to implement and verify this documentation update.

Issues.Title: Make tool-command resolution and invocation AOT-able
Issues.Body:
Pull external/tool-command resolution and invocation into the NativeAOT bridge ([`NativeEntryPoint`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet-aot/NativeEntryPoint.cs)) so commands like global/local tools, PATH commands, and app-base commands resolve and run out-of-process from the AOT fast path, while keeping the managed implementations as the fallback. Resolution and invocation: - Add [`TryInvokeExternalCommand`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet-aot/NativeEntryPoint.cs#L189) to `NativeEntryPoint`: resolve via the AOT-safe resolver set and invoke out-of-process; defer file-based apps, legacy project tools, unresolved commands, and any resolution error to the managed CLI. Switch the dispatch to [`ParseResult.CanBeInvoked()`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet/Extensions/ParseResultExtensions.cs#L136). - Thread an `sdkRoot` through [`CommandResolver`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet/CommandFactory/CommandResolver.cs) / [`ICommandResolverPolicy`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet/CommandFactory/CommandResolution/ICommandResolverPolicy.cs) / [`DefaultCommandResolverPolicy`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet/CommandFactory/CommandResolution/DefaultCommandResolverPolicy.cs) and add [`DotnetToolsCommandResolver.ForSdkRoot`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet/CommandFactory/CommandResolution/DotnetToolsCommandResolver.cs#L12), since the AOT app's `AppContext.BaseDirectory` is the dotnet root rather than the per-SDK version path. - Gate [`ProjectToolsCommandResolver`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet/CommandFactory/CommandResolution/ProjectToolsCommandResolver.cs) and [`PackagedCommandSpecFactoryWithCliRuntime`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet/CommandFactory/CommandResolution/PackagedCommandSpecFactoryWithCliRuntime.cs) (MSBuild/NuGet-based, AOT-hostile) out of the resolver set under `!CLI_AOT`. - Add resolution telemetry tags to [`CompositeCommandResolver`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet/CommandFactory/CommandResolution/CompositeCommandResolver.cs) and the tool resolvers. AOT compatibility: - Annotate MSBuild Object Model code paths with `[RequiresDynamicCode]` and guard in-proc MSBuild usage with `RuntimeFeature.IsDynamicCodeSupported` ([`RunCommand`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet/Commands/Run/RunCommand.cs), [`RunProperties`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet/Commands/Run/RunProperties.cs), [`VirtualProjectBuildingCommand`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet/Commands/Run/VirtualProjectBuildingCommand.cs), [`DotNetCommandFactory`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet/Commands/DotNetCommandFactory.cs), [`MSBuildForwardingAppWithoutLogging`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/Microsoft.DotNet.Cli.Utils/MSBuildForwardingAppWithoutLogging.cs), Pack/Publish/Project/Run-Api/Test MTP). - Gate MSBuild usings and [`IProject`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet/CommandFactory/CommandResolution/IProject.cs) usage out of the AOT build; duplicate [`IsValidEntryPointPath`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet/Extensions/ParseResultExtensions.cs#L57) into [`ParseResultExtensions`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet/Extensions/ParseResultExtensions.cs) to avoid pulling MSBuild onto the AOT codepath. - Expose [`IFileSystem`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/Microsoft.DotNet.InternalAbstractions/IFileSystem.cs) internals to [`dotnet-aot`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/tree/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet-aot) via `InternalsVisibleTo`. - Add the AOT-safe `CommandFactory`/tool-resolution sources to [`AotSourceFiles.props`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet-aot/AotSourceFiles.props). Telemetry: - Unify activity/span names across the managed and AOT resolution paths so traces from each host can be compared directly. - Resolution telemetry records command, resolution status (`found`/`notfound`), and resolver-specific tags; environment variables and argument lists are intentionally not tagged (secrets / unbounded size). - Add [`eng/gather-otel.ps1`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/eng/gather-otel.ps1) and [`eng/gather-otel.sh`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/eng/gather-otel.sh) helper scripts to capture OpenTelemetry data from a run for validating that the AOT and managed paths emit equivalent telemetry. Tests and docs: - Add `NativeEntryPoint` tests for unresolved external command and resolvable PATH command paths. - Document the external-command resolution path and updated dispatch flow in [`DESIGN.md`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet-aot/DESIGN.md). Performance: Measured with [`eng/gather-otel.ps1`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/eng/gather-otel.ps1) (5 managed + 5 AOT runs each) on a Debug build, end-to-end wall-clock per invocation pulled from the dashboard telemetry. Two tools that resolve via different resolvers: `dotnet dev-certs https` — bundled DotnetTools tool (resolves at the 2nd resolver, [`DotnetToolsCommandResolver`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet/CommandFactory/CommandResolution/DotnetToolsCommandResolver.cs)): | | mean | median | min | max | |---|---|---|---|---| | managed | 701.4 ms | 685 ms | 648 ms | 815 ms | | AOT | 219.2 ms | 220 ms | 215 ms | 223 ms |
`dotnet dev-certs https` / managed trace
`dotnet dev-certs https` / NAOT trace
→ −482 ms, 3.2x faster, 68.7% lower latency. `dotnet ef` — PATH / global tool (resolves at the last resolver; the AOT chain has one fewer resolver because [`ProjectToolsCommandResolver`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/fa2346f637e8b7e1db899497d150531ef9c0ad8f/src/Cli/dotnet/CommandFactory/CommandResolution/ProjectToolsCommandResolver.cs) is gated out under `!CLI_AOT`): | | mean | median | min | max | |---|---|---|---|---| | managed | 703.4 ms | 689 ms | 661 ms | 768 ms | | AOT | 199.0 ms | 197 ms | 195 ms | 205 ms |
`dotnet ef` / managed trace
`dotnet ef` / NAOT trace
→ −504 ms, 3.53x faster, 71.7% lower latency. The win comes from not booting CoreCLR and re-running the managed CLI a second time (~600-690 ms of the `coreclr-invocation` span); the actual tool launch (~85-135 ms, the `execute-extensible-command` span) is shared and identical on both paths.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Make tool-command resolution and invocation AOT-able" (#54810)

Issues.Title: File-based apps: `dotnet build --file` behaves inconsistently with `dotnet run --file`
Issues.Body:
Repro:

1) Open some directory containing a file-based app `app.cs`.
2) Use `dotnet run --file app.cs`. Observe that it runs.
3) Use `dotnet build --file app.cs`. Observe output like the following:

```sh
> dotnet build --file app.cs                                                               
Warning: 'app.cs' appears to be a file-based app but was treated as an MSBuild argument. To treat it as a file-based app, use 'dotnet build app.cs'.
MSBUILD : error MSB1001: Unknown switch.
    Full command line: '...'
  Switches appended by response files:
Switch: --file
```

I imagine the current behavior is already known and there are reasons for the behavior.

I just thought I would raise the ergonomic concern anyway. I would like to be able to just be able to pass `--file <path>` to whatever command, whether it's `dotnet run`, `build`, `restore`, or something else, to make sure a file-based app is used. The difference in support across commands is a "decoder ring".
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "File-based apps: `dotnet build --file` behaves inconsistently with `dotnet run --file`" (#54808)

Issues.Title: Fix solution file discovery to prefer local .slnx over ancestor .sln in post-actions
Issues.Body:
## Summary

When `dotnet new` runs the "add project to solution" post-action, it searches for a solution file starting from the output directory and walking up the parent hierarchy. Previously, the search looked for `*.sln` across the **entire** ancestor chain first, and only fell back to `*.slnx` if no `.sln` was found anywhere. This meant a `.slnx` in the output directory would be ignored if any `.sln` existed in an ancestor directory.

Since `dotnet new sln` now creates `.slnx` files by default, this caused incorrect behavior: the post-action would target an unrelated `.sln` in a parent directory instead of the intended local `.slnx`.

## Fix

Changed `FindSolutionFilesAtOrAbovePath` to search for both `.sln` and `.slnx` at **each directory level** before traversing to the parent, while still preferring `.sln` over `.slnx` within the same directory.

## Observable difference

| Scenario | Old behavior | New behavior |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `.slnx` in output dir, `.sln` in ancestor dir | Finds the **ancestor's `.sln`** (ignores local `.slnx`) | Finds the **local `.slnx`** ✅ |
| `.sln` in output dir only | Same — finds it | Same |
| `.slnx` in output dir only, no `.sln` anywhere | Same — finds it | Same |
| `.sln` and `.slnx` in same dir | Same — prefers `.sln` | Same |

## The one scenario that changes

A user runs `dotnet new <template>` in a directory that has a `.slnx` file, but an ancestor directory also has a `.sln` file. Previously, the project would be added to the **ancestor's `.sln`**. Now it's added to the **local `.slnx`**.

A user with a `.slnx` in their working directory almost certainly intends for the new project to be added there, not to some unrelated `.sln` higher up the tree. The old behavior was an unintended consequence of the two-pass search approach when `.slnx` support was added.

## Discovery

This was discovered via intermittent CI test failures where a stray `.sln` file in the Helix temp directory hierarchy was found by the ancestor search, causing the post-action to target a file that was subsequently deleted by another parallel test:
- [Build 1466643](https://dev.azure.com/dnceng-public/public/_build/results?buildId=1466643)
- [Build 1466026](https://dev.azure.com/dnceng-public/public/_build/results?buildId=1466026&view=ms.vss-test-web.build-test-results-tab&runId=40527762&resultId=113154)

## Test

Added `AddProjectToSolutionPostActionPrefersLocalSlnxOverParentSln` — a regression test that places a `.sln` in a parent directory and a `.slnx` in the output directory, verifying the local `.slnx` is found.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Fix solution file discovery to prefer local .slnx over ancestor .sln in post-actions" (#54805)

Issues.Title: NativeAOT CLI: file-based app detection throws FileNotFoundException for 'Microsoft.Build' under AOT
Issues.Body:
### Summary

The shared CLI parser path `ParseResultExtensions.GetFileBasedAppEntryPointToken` (added in #54653) calls `VirtualProjectBuilder.IsValidEntryPointPath`, which pulls in the `Microsoft.Build` assembly. `Microsoft.Build` cannot be linked into a NativeAOT image, so this method **always throws** `System.IO.FileNotFoundException: Could not find file 'Microsoft.Build'` when compiled with NativeAOT.

The NativeAOT CLI entry point (`dn` / `NativeEntryPoint`) calls `GetFileBasedAppEntryPointToken` to detect and defer `dotnet app.cs` file-based app invocations (see the remarks on the method), so this affects the real AOT CLI, not just the tests.

### Evidence

The NativeAOT compiler (ILC) flags it at publish time:

```
ILC: Method '[dotnet-aot.Tests]Microsoft.DotNet.Cli.Extensions.ParseResultExtensions.GetFileBasedAppEntryPointToken(ParseResult)'
  will always throw because: Failed to load assembly 'Microsoft.Build'
ILC: Method '[Microsoft.DotNet.Cli.Utils]Microsoft.DotNet.Cli.Utils.MSBuildForwardingAppWithoutLogging.ExecuteInProc(string[])'
  will always throw because: Failed to load assembly 'Microsoft.Build'
```

At runtime the AOT binary then throws:

```
System.IO.FileNotFoundException : Could not find file 'Microsoft.Build'.
  at Internal.Runtime.TypeLoaderExceptionHelper.CreateFileNotFoundException(ExceptionStringID, String)
  at Microsoft.DotNet.Cli.Extensions.ParseResultExtensions.GetFileBasedAppEntryPointToken(ParseResult)
```

### Affected tests (quarantined under AOT pending this fix)

These are skipped via the `Skip` parameter pointing at this issue in `test/dotnet-aot.Tests/AotParserTests.cs`:

- `AotParserTests.DetectFileBasedApp_WhenFirstArgIsCSharpFile`
- `AotParserTests.DoesNotDetectFileBasedApp_ForBuiltInCommand`
- `AotParserTests.DoesNotDetectFileBasedApp_ForNonExistentFile`

### Context

Surfaced by the NativeAOT CLI test CI hookup (#54719), which is the first time `test/dotnet-aot.Tests` runs under NativeAOT in CI. Once file-based app detection is made AOT-safe (so it no longer requires `Microsoft.Build`), the `Skip` annotations should be removed.

cc @baronfel
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "NativeAOT CLI: file-based app detection throws FileNotFoundException for 'Microsoft.Build' under AOT" (#54806)

Issues.Title: `dotnetup` full replacement mode on Mac should set other configuration for GUI apps
Issues.Body:
### Prerequisites

- [x] I have read the [dotnetup documentation](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/tree/release/dnup/documentation/general/dotnetup).
- [x] I have searched for [existing dotnetup issues](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues?q=is%3Aissue%20state%3Aopen%20label%3Adotnetup).
- [x] I have searched for [existing dotnetup discussions](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/discussions/categories/dotnetup).

### Issue type

Bug report

### Description

The issue with mac is that env vars set in the terminal don't impact applications launched through the GUI (e.g. the dock) unless they are also added in special root owned locations.

In full replacement mode on Mac, we should likely also set the environment variables for `DOTNET_ROOT` and perhaps append to `PATH` in `/etc/launchd.conf`. 

We could also consider writing the entry within `paths.d` like the `pkg` installer does, however we'd need to consider if that causes a 'break' or not. However, both of those require elevation so dotnetup would need to be elevated/run under sudo.

For apps like VS Code, we should consider how 'terminal' mode may be confusing to users when it does not get picked up via vscode when launched from the Dock.

### dotnetup version

_No response_
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "`dotnetup` full replacement mode on Mac should set other configuration for GUI apps" (#54803)

Issues.Title: .NET 11 Preview 5
Issues.Body:
> This issue is a locked mirror of https://github.com/dotnet/core/discussions/10445. See that thread for discussion.

We are pleased to announce the release of .NET 11 Preview 5. Follow the main announcement and [blog announcement](https://aka.ms/dotnet/11/preview5).

Browse the release notes for a full list of improvements in this release:

- [.NET MAUI](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/dotnetmaui.md)
- [ASP.NET Core](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/aspnetcore.md)
- [C#](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/csharp.md)
- [F#](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/fsharp.md)
- [Container images](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/containers.md)
- [EF Core & Data](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/efcore.md)
- [Libraries](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/libraries.md)
- [MSBuild](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/msbuild.md)
- [NuGet](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/nuget.md)
- [Runtime](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/runtime.md)
- [SDK](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/sdk.md)
- [Windows Forms](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/winforms.md)
- [WPF](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/wpf.md)

## Get started

To get started with .NET 11, [install the .NET 11 SDK](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet/11.0).

If you're on Windows using Visual Studio, we recommend installing the latest [Visual Studio 2026 Insiders](https://visualstudio.microsoft.com/insiders/). You can also use Visual Studio Code and the [C# Dev Kit](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=ms-dotnettools.csdevkit) extension with .NET 11.

Issues.Body: ## Executive summary Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in ASP.NET Core SignalR and Blazor Server. This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. A denial of service vulnerability exists in the MessagePack hub protocol used by SignalR and Blazor Server where an attacker can send deeply-nested MessagePack arrays to cause a stack overflow, resulting in a denial of service. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/discussions/67100 ## CVSS Details - **Version:** 3.1 - **Severity:** High [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.osx-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.osx-x64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x86](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x86) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 ## Advisory FAQ ### How do I know if I am affected? If using a package listed in [affected packages](#affected-packages), you're exposed to the vulnerability. ### How do I fix the issue? 1. To fix the issue please install the latest version of .NET 10.0, .NET 9.0, or .NET 8.0. If you have installed one or more .NET SDKs through Visual Studio, Visual Studio will prompt you to update Visual Studio, which will also update your .NET SDKs. 2. If your application references the vulnerable package, update the package reference to the patched version. You can list the versions you have installed by running the `dotnet --info` command. * If you're using .NET 8.0, you should download and install .NET 8.0.28 Runtime or .NET 8.0.xxx SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/8.0. * If you're using .NET 9.0, you should download and install .NET 9.0.17 Runtime or .NET 9.0.xxx SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/9.0. * If you're using .NET 10.0, you should download and install .NET 10.0.9 Runtime or .NET 10.0.xxx SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/10.0. Once you have installed the updated runtime or SDK, restart your apps for the update to take effect. Additionally, if you've deployed [self-contained applications](https://docs.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/deploying/#self-contained-deployments-scd) targeting any of the impacted versions, these applications are also vulnerable and must be recompiled and redeployed. ## Other Information ### Reporting Security Issues If you have found a potential security issue in a supported version of .NET, please report it to the Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC) via the [MSRC Researcher Portal](https://msrc.microsoft.com/report/vulnerability/new). Further information can be found in the MSRC [Report an Issue FAQ](https://www.microsoft.com/msrc/faqs-report-an-issue). Security reports made through MSRC may qualify for the Microsoft .NET Bounty. Details of the Microsoft .NET Bounty Program including terms and conditions are at https://aka.ms/corebounty. ### Support You can ask questions about this issue on GitHub in the .NET GitHub organization. The main repos are located at https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore. The Announcements repo (https://github.com/dotnet/Announcements) will contain this bulletin as an issue and will include a link to a discussion issue. You can ask questions in the linked discussion issue. ### Disclaimer The information provided in this advisory is provided "as is" without warranty of any kind. Microsoft disclaims all warranties, either express or implied, including the warranties of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose. In no event shall Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers be liable for any damages whatsoever including direct, indirect, incidental, consequential, loss of business profits or special damages, even if Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers have been advised of the possibility of such damages. Some states do not allow the exclusion or limitation of liability for consequential or incidental damages so the foregoing limitation may not apply. ### External Links [CVE-2026-45591]( https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2026-45591) ### Acknowledgements Anonymous ### Revisions V1.0 (June 9, 2026): Advisory published.

Issues.Title: Microsoft Security Advisory CVE-2026-45491 | .NET Tampering Vulnerability
Issues.Body: ## Executive summary Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in System.Formats.Tar. This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. A tampering vulnerability exists in the `TarFile.ExtractToDirectory` method where a symlink path traversal enables an attacker to perform arbitrary file writes outside the intended extraction directory. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/issues/129184 ## CVSS Details - **Version:** 3.1 - **Severity:** Medium - **Score:** 6.8 - **Vector:** `CVSS:3.1/AV:L/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:L` - **Weakness:** CWE-59 (Improper Link Resolution Before File Access) ## Affected Platforms - **Platforms:** All - **Architectures:** All ## [Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.linux-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.linux-x64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.osx-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.osx-arm64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.osx-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.osx-x64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.win-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.win-x64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 [Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.win-x86](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.NetCore.App.Runtime.win-x86) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 ## Advisory FAQ ### How do I know if I am affected? If using a package listed in [affected packages](#affected-packages), you're exposed to the vulnerability. ### How do I fix the issue? 1. To fix the issue please install the latest version of .NET 10.0, .NET 9.0, or .NET 8.0. If you have installed one or more .NET SDKs through Visual Studio, Visual Studio will prompt you to update Visual Studio, which will also update your .NET SDKs. 2. If your application references the vulnerable package, update the package reference to the patched version. You can list the versions you have installed by running the `dotnet --info` command. * If you're using .NET 8.0, you should download and install .NET 8.0.28 Runtime or .NET 8.0.xxx SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/8.0. * If you're using .NET 9.0, you should download and install .NET 9.0.17 Runtime or .NET 9.0.xxx SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/9.0. * If you're using .NET 10.0, you should download and install .NET 10.0.9 Runtime or .NET 10.0.xxx SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/10.0. Once you have installed the updated runtime or SDK, restart your apps for the update to take effect. Additionally, if you've deployed [self-contained applications](https://docs.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/deploying/#self-contained-deployments-scd) targeting any of the impacted versions, these applications are also vulnerable and must be recompiled and redeployed. ## Other Information ### Reporting Security Issues If you have found a potential security issue in a supported version of .NET, please report it to the Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC) via the [MSRC Researcher Portal](https://msrc.microsoft.com/report/vulnerability/new). Further information can be found in the MSRC [Report an Issue FAQ](https://www.microsoft.com/msrc/faqs-report-an-issue). Security reports made through MSRC may qualify for the Microsoft .NET Bounty. Details of the Microsoft .NET Bounty Program including terms and conditions are at https://aka.ms/corebounty. ### Support You can ask questions about this issue on GitHub in the .NET GitHub organization. The main repos are located at https://github.com/dotnet/runtime. The Announcements repo (https://github.com/dotnet/Announcements) will contain this bulletin as an issue and will include a link to a discussion issue. You can ask questions in the linked discussion issue. ### Disclaimer The information provided in this advisory is provided "as is" without warranty of any kind. In no event shall Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers be liable for any damages whatsoever including direct, indirect, incidental, consequential, loss of business profits or special damages, even if Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers have been advised of the possibility of such damages. Some states do not allow the exclusion or limitation of liability for consequential or incidental damages so the foregoing limitation may not apply. ### External Links [CVE-2026-45491]( https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2026-45491) ### Acknowledgements Anonymous, Ashmit Sharma, Ky0toFu, phrolo7a ### Revisions V1.0 (June 9, 2026): Advisory published.

Issues.Title: Microsoft Security Advisory CVE-2026-45490 | .NET SDK Elevation of Privilege Vulnerability
Issues.Body: ## Executive summary Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in .NET 8.0, .NET 9.0, and .NET 10.0. This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their environments to remove this vulnerability. An elevation of privilege vulnerability exists in the .NET SDK `dotnet.exe` workload command where one local user can exploit a weakly ACLed named pipe to perform arbitrary file creation or truncation as another local user. This only affects .NET on Windows operating systems. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/54652 ## Mitigation factors Microsoft has not identified any mitigating factors for this vulnerability. ## Affected software * .NET 10.0 SDK |Affected versions|Patched versions| |-----------------|----------------| |>= 10.0.100, <= 10.0.108|10.0.109| |>= 10.0.300, <= 10.0.300|10.0.301| * .NET 9.0 SDK |Affected versions|Patched versions| |-----------------|----------------| |>= 9.0.100, <= 9.0.117|9.0.118| |>= 9.0.300, <= 9.0.314|9.0.315| * .NET 8.0 SDK |Affected versions|Patched versions| |-----------------|----------------| |>= 8.0.100, <= 8.0.127|8.0.128| |>= 8.0.400, <= 8.0.421|8.0.422| ## Affected Packages The vulnerability does not affect Microsoft .NET packages. ## Advisory FAQ ### How do I know if I am affected? If you have an SDK with a version listed, you're exposed to the vulnerability. ### How do I fix the issue? 1. To fix the issue please install the latest version of .NET 8.0 SDK, .NET 9.0 SDK, or .NET 10.0 SDK, as appropriate. * If you're using .NET 8.0, you should download and install the latest .NET 8.0 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/8.0. * If you're using .NET 9.0, you should download and install the latest .NET 9.0 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/9.0. * If you're using .NET 10.0, you should download and install the latest .NET 10.0 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/10.0. * You can list the versions you have installed by running the `dotnet --info` command. You will see output like the following; ``` .NET SDK: Version: 10.0.100 Commit: 59db016f11 Runtime Environment: OS Name: Windows OS Version: 10.0.26100 OS Platform: Windows RID: win-x64 Base Path: C:\Program Files\dotnet\sdk\10.0.100\ .NET SDKs installed: 10.0.100 [C:\Program Files\dotnet\sdk] .NET runtimes installed: Microsoft.AspNetCore.App 10.0.0 [C:\Program Files\dotnet\shared\Microsoft.AspNetCore.App] Microsoft.NETCore.App 10.0.0 [C:\Program Files\dotnet\shared\Microsoft.NETCore.App] ``` Once you have installed the updated SDK, close any open command prompts, terminals, IDEs, or build agents that may have loaded an older `dotnet.exe`, and then re-run the affected `dotnet workload` command using the updated SDK. ## Other Information ### Reporting Security Issues If you have found a potential security issue in .NET 8.0, .NET 9.0, or .NET 10.0, please email details to [secure@microsoft.com](mailto:secure@microsoft.com). Reports may qualify for the Microsoft .NET Core & .NET 5 Bounty. Details of the Microsoft .NET Bounty Program including terms and conditions are at https://aka.ms/corebounty. ### Support You can ask questions about this issue on GitHub in the .NET GitHub organization. The main repos are located at https://github.com/dotnet/sdk. The Announcements repo (https://github.com/dotnet/Announcements) will contain this bulletin as an issue and will include a link to a discussion issue. You can ask questions in the linked discussion issue. ### Disclaimer The information provided in this advisory is provided "as is" without warranty of any kind. Microsoft disclaims all warranties, either express or implied, including the warranties of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose. In no event shall Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers be liable for any damages whatsoever including direct, indirect, incidental, consequential, loss of business profits or special damages, even if Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers have been advised of the possibility of such damages. Some states do not allow the exclusion or limitation of liability for consequential or incidental damages so the foregoing limitation may not apply. ### External Links [CVE-2026-45490](https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2026-45490) ### Acknowledgements Ky0toFu ### Revisions V1.0 (June 9, 2026): Advisory published.

Issues.Title: .NET 11 Preview 4
Issues.Body:
> This issue is a locked mirror of https://github.com/dotnet/core/discussions/10415. See that thread for discussion.

We are pleased to announce the release of .NET 11 Preview 4. Follow the main announcement and [blog announcement](https://aka.ms/dotnet/11/preview4).

Browse the release notes for a full list of improvements in this release:

- [.NET MAUI](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/dotnetmaui.md)
- [ASP.NET Core](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/aspnetcore.md)
- [C#](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/csharp.md)
- [F#](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/fsharp.md)
- [Container images](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/containers.md)
- [EF Core & Data](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/efcore.md)
- [Libraries](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/libraries.md)
- [MSBuild](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/msbuild.md)
- [NuGet](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/nuget.md)
- [Runtime](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/runtime.md)
- [SDK](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/sdk.md)
- [Windows Forms](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/winforms.md)
- [WPF](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/wpf.md)

## Get started

To get started with .NET 11, [install the .NET 11 SDK](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet/11.0).

If you're on Windows using Visual Studio, we recommend installing the latest [Visual Studio 2026 Insiders](https://visualstudio.microsoft.com/insiders/). You can also use Visual Studio Code and the [C# Dev Kit](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=ms-dotnettools.csdevkit) extension with .NET 11.

Issues.Title: Microsoft Security Advisory CVE-2026-32177 – .NET Elevation of Privilege Vulnerability
Issues.Body: ## Executive Summary: Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in .NET 8.0, .NET 9.0, and .NET 10.0. This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. Heap-based buffer overflow in .NET allows an unauthorized attacker to elevate privileges locally. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/wpf/issues/11632 ## CVSS Details - **Version:** 3.1 - **Severity:** High - **Score:** 7.3 - **Vector:** AV:L/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:L/E:U/RL:O/RC:C - **Weakness:** CWE-122: Heap-based Buffer Overflow, CWE-20: Improper Input Validation ## Affected Platforms - **Platforms:** Windows - **Architectures:** All ## Affected Packages The vulnerability affects any Microsoft .NET project if it uses any of affected package versions listed below ### .NET 10 Package name | Affected version | Patched version ------------ | ---------------- | ------------------------- [Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-arm64) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.7 | 10.0.8 [MMicrosoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x64) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.7 | 10.0.8 [Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x86](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x86) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.7 | 10.0.8 ### .NET 9 Package name | Affected version | Patched version ------------ | ---------------- | ------------------------- [Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-arm64) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.15 | 9.0.16 [MMicrosoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x64) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.15 | 9.0.16 [Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x86](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x86) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.15 | 9.0.16 ### .NET 8 Package name | Affected version | Patched version ------------ | ---------------- | ------------------------- [Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-arm64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.26 | 8.0.27 [MMicrosoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.26 | 8.0.27 [Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x86](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x86) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.26 | 8.0.27 ## Advisory FAQ ### How do I know if I am affected? If using a package listed in [affected packages](#affected-packages), you're exposed to the vulnerability. ### How do I fix the issue? 1. To fix the issue please install the latest version of .NET 8.0, NET 9.0, or .NET 10.0, as appropriate. If you have installed one or more .NET SDKs through Visual Studio, Visual Studio will prompt you to update Visual Studio, which will also update your .NET SDKs. 2. If your application references the vulnerable package, update the package reference to the patched version. You can list the versions you have installed by running the `dotnet --info` command. Once you have installed the updated runtime or SDK, restart your apps for the update to take effect. Additionally, if you've deployed [self-contained applications](https://docs.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/deploying/#self-contained-deployments-scd) targeting any of the impacted versions, these applications are also vulnerable and must be recompiled and redeployed. ## Other Information ### Reporting Security Issues If you have found a potential security issue in a supported version of .NET, please report it to the Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC) via the [MSRC Researcher Portal](https://msrc.microsoft.com/report/vulnerability/new). Further information can be found in the MSRC [Report an Issue FAQ](https://www.microsoft.com/msrc/faqs-report-an-issue). Security reports made through MSRC may qualify for the Microsoft .NET Bounty. Details of the Microsoft .NET Bounty Program including terms and conditions are at https://aka.ms/corebounty. ### Support You can ask questions about this issue on GitHub in the .NET GitHub organization. The main repos are located at https://github.com/dotnet/runtime. The Announcements repo (https://github.com/dotnet/Announcements) will contain this bulletin as an issue and will include a link to a discussion issue. You can ask questions in the linked discussion issue. ### Disclaimer The information provided in this advisory is provided "as is" without warranty of any kind. Microsoft disclaims all warranties, either express or implied, including the warranties of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose. In no event shall Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers be liable for any damages whatsoever including direct, indirect, incidental, consequential, loss of business profits or special damages, even if Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers have been advised of the possibility of such damages. Some states do not allow the exclusion or limitation of liability for consequential or incidental damages so the foregoing limitation may not apply. ### External Links [CVE-2026-32177]( https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2026-32177) ### Acknowledgements Ky0toFu ### Revisions V1.0 (May 12, 2026): Advisory published.

Issues.Title: Microsoft Security Advisory CVE-2026-42899 – ASP.NET Core Denial of Service Vulnerability
Issues.Body: ## Executive Summary: Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in .NET 8.0, .NET 9.0, and .NET 10.0. This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. Loop with unreachable exit condition ('infinite loop') in ASP.NET Core allows an unauthorized attacker to deny service over a network. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/discussions/66659 ## CVSS Details - **Version:** 3.1 - **Severity:** - **Score:** 7.5 - **Vector:** AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H/E:U/RL:O/RC:C - **Weakness:** CWE-835: Loop with Unreachable Exit Condition ('Infinite Loop') ## Affected Platforms - **Platforms:** All - **Architectures:** All ## Affected Packages The vulnerability affects any If using a package listed in [affected packages](#affected-packages), you're exposed to the vulnerability. ### How do I fix the issue? 1. To fix the issue please install the latest version of .NET 8.0, NET 9.0, or .NET 10.0, as appropriate. If you have installed one or more .NET SDKs through Visual Studio, Visual Studio will prompt you to update Visual Studio, which will also update your .NET SDKs. 2. If your application references the vulnerable package, update the package reference to the patched version. You can list the versions you have installed by running the `dotnet --info` command. Once you have installed the updated runtime or SDK, restart your apps for the update to take effect. Further information can be found in the MSRC [Report an Issue FAQ](https://www.microsoft.com/msrc/faqs-report-an-issue). Security reports made through MSRC may qualify for the Microsoft .NET Bounty. Details of the Microsoft .NET Bounty Program including terms and conditions are at https://aka.ms/corebounty. ### Support You can ask questions about this issue on GitHub in the .NET GitHub organization. The main repos are located at https://github.com/dotnet/runtime. The Announcements repo (https://github.com/dotnet/Announcements) will contain this bulletin as an issue and will include a link to a discussion issue. You can ask questions in the linked discussion issue. ### Disclaimer The information provided in this advisory is provided "as is" without warranty of any kind. Issues.Title: Microsoft Security Advisory CVE-2026-32175 – .NET Core Tampering Vulnerability
Issues.Body: ## Executive Summary: Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in .NET 8.0, .NET 9.0, and .NET 10.0. This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. A tampering vulnerability exists when .NET Core improperly handles specially crafted files. An attacker who successfully exploited this vulnerability could write arbitrary files and directories to certain locations on a vulnerable system. However, an attacker would have limited control over the destination of the files and directories. To exploit the vulnerability, an attacker must send a specially crafted file to a vulnerable system. The security update fixes the vulnerability by ensuring .NET Core properly handles files. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/issues/128089 ## CVSS Details - **Version:** 3.1 - **Severity:** - **Score:** 7.5 - **Vector:** /AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:N - **Weakness:** CWE-36: Absolute Path Traversal ## Affected Platforms -
